name: Britt Woodman
abstract: Britt Woodman (June 4, 1920 – October 13, 2000) was an American jazz trombonist.

concerts.label: Midnight Performance: Dizzy Gillespie: Genius at Midnight
Tribute to Charles Mingus: Mingus Music
Kool Jazz Festival: Women Blow Their Own Horns
Kool Jazz Festival: Ellington's Black, Brown and Beige
Kool Jazz Festival: A Night of Genius-Artists Celebrate Mingus
JVC Jazz Festival: Wynton Marsalis Salutes Dizzy Gillespie on His Seventieth Birthday
JVC Jazz Festival: An Evening with Mel Tormé
JVC Jazz Festival: A Tribute to Reverend John Garcia Bensel, The Pastor of the Jazz Community

about_quotes.speaker: Art Farmer
about_quotes.evidence: Britt Woodman, I heard him play. ... It was a great band, but I didn’t meet the guys.
about_quotes.doc_title: Art Farmer — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Wilder Part 1
about_quotes.evidence: The first big band I played with... was Les Hite... Britt Woodman was the first trombone player... Gerald Wiggins joined the band. He was in the band at the time Dizzy Gillespie was in the band for a short time.
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Wilder Part 1 interviewed by Monk Rowe, Sarasota, Florida, April 12, 1996

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Owens
about_quotes.evidence: I was actually with Mingus two times... That was Rolf Ericson, Lonnie Hillyer and myself in the trumpet section. Britt Woodman played trombone... We worked at Birdland for two weeks
about_quotes.doc_title: Jimmy Owens — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: they had a band together down at the Downbeat Club with John Anderson and Buddy Collette and Red Callender... and Britt Woodman on trombone.

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Wilder
about_quotes.evidence: I joined them in Lansing, Michigan... Britt Woodman, the trombonist, he was first trombone player... They were a nice bunch of fellows.
about_quotes.doc_title: Joe Wilder — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: I played with him with Nelson Riddle, and then Nat Cole and did a few recordings and with Duke Ellington
